microblogs.title: How the Rubik Sketch Works
microblogs.snippet:
The sketch draws a Rubik's cube entirely on a 2D canvas using isometric projection. It supports
sizes from 2×2 to 7×7, scrambles the state with random face turns, and plays back a solve
by reversing those same moves in o

microblogs.title: How the Turing Patterns Sketch Grows Living Texture
microblogs.snippet: The Turing Patterns sketch runs on a reaction-diffusion system where most of the visible complexity comes from one compact loop repeated at pixel scale. Each pixel tracks two concentrations, A and B, and each frame appli

microblogs.title: The Fourier Pendulum That Draws a Wave
microblogs.snippet: This sketch feels like a pendulum machine built out of circles. Each circle spins at its own speed. The tip of the last circle traces a point. Then that point gets projected to the right, where it becomes a moving wavefo

microblogs.title: Four Numbers, a Million Points, and the Clifford Attractor
microblogs.snippet: Pick four decimal numbers. Call them a, b, c, d. Start at the origin. Run this loop 800,000 times:

microblogs.title: Deterministic chaos and chemical memory
microblogs.snippet:
Two visualizations from the gallery that sit at opposite ends of the same idea: systems
      that remember nothing, yet produce structures that look designed.

In 1965 A. Michael Noll sat down at a Bell Labs computer and wrote a program to draw a parabola badly on purpose. The machine plotted a quadratic curve, then scattered each point with Gaussian noise before connecting the

microblogs.title: The wave that isn't there
microblogs.snippet: 24 uncoupled pendulums swinging at arithmetic frequencies. No pendulum talks to any other. The wave pattern is entirely in your eye.

microblogs.title: Tuning forks and pixel dust
microblogs.snippet: In 1855, Jules Antoine Lissajous attached tiny mirrors to two tuning forks, aimed a beam of light at each, and let them vibrate at right angles. The reflected light traced closed curves on a screen: circles when the freq

microblogs.title: What a flock doesn't know
microblogs.snippet: 120 boids, three rules, no flock-level state. Click to reset; move mouse to pull the swarm.

microblogs.title: The angle that doesn't repeat
microblogs.snippet: 1,400 dots placed at 137.5° increments, radius scaling as √index. Click to pulse. Move mouse to slow the rotation.

microblogs.title: Sand knows where to sit
microblogs.snippet: Sand-colored nodal lines on a virtual plate. Click or tap to advance through mode pairs (m, n). The pattern morphs between states with a cubic ease.

microblogs.title: The first art machine
microblogs.snippet: Four coupled pendulums trace a decaying Lissajous figure in sepia ink. Click or tap to reset with new frequencies.

microblogs.title: The grammar that grows a forest
microblogs.snippet: Seven presets: fractal tree, fern, Koch snowflake, Dragon curve, Sierpiński triangle, bush, Hilbert curve. Space cycles presets; arrow keys adjust depth and angle.

microblogs.title: Space carved by proximity
microblogs.snippet: 30 drifting seeds carve the canvas by nearest-neighbor ownership. Click to add seeds, drag to push, R to reset.

microblogs.title: Two chemicals, a thousand patterns
microblogs.snippet: Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion. Click to seed new chemical clusters; press space to cycle through five presets (Coral, Mitosis, Spots, Maze, Waves).
